narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid LSAT score of 39 and an undergraduate GPA of 3.10, you've clearly put in the effort to build a strong academic foundation, including your choice to pursue full-time studies. Your family comes from a middle-income background, which is common among many applicants. However, the university's admission model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your risk of failure at 56.7%—just over the threshold that led to your application's rejection. This prediction was driven primarily by your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, which placed you in the lower end compared to the typical student's midpoint of around 5.5, strongly pushing the model toward foreseeing a bar exam struggle. Your Year 1 decile of 2 and middle family income also contributed somewhat to this concern, as they signaled potential hurdles relative to peers who often rank higher. On a brighter note, your standout LSAT score had the strongest positive pull, nudging the prediction toward success more than any other factor, while your undergraduate GPA and full-time commitment provided additional support. Compared to the average applicant—who has Year 1 and 3 deciles around 5.7 and 5.5, an LSAT of 36.7, and a slightly higher GPA of 3.21—your test performance shines, but your school rankings stood out as the biggest drags. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a lawyer; it's the model's data-driven assessment based on patterns from past students. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your school decile rankings, as those proved most influential here, and perhaps seek targeted bar prep resources to align even better with successful profiles. You're close—many applicants in similar spots have improved their standings and succeeded. Keep pushing; your LSAT strength shows you have what it takes. We believe in your ability to turn this around.
